questions.question: Explain the Rule of Sum and Rule of Product with suitable examples.
keyPoints: Rule of Sum: If tasks are mutually exclusive (one OR the other), add the number of ways. Example: Choosing between 3 red and 4 blue shirts = 3 + 4 = 7 ways.
Rule of Product: If tasks are sequential/independent (one AND then another), multiply the number of ways. Example: Choosing a shirt (3) and then pants (4) = 3 × 4 = 12 outfits.
Emphasize 'OR' vs 'AND' in problem statements

questions.question: What is the difference between a permutation and a combination? Give one example of each.
keyPoints: Permutation: Arrangement where order matters. Example: Arranging 3 books on a shelf from 5 books = 5P3 = 60 ways.
Combination: Selection where order doesn't matter. Example: Selecting 3 committee members from 10 people = 10C3 = 120 ways.
Key difference: nPr = nCr × r!

questions.question: State and explain the Pigeonhole Principle with an example.
keyPoints: If n items are placed into m containers and n > m, then at least one container contains more than one item.
Formal: If n items are placed into k boxes, at least one box contains at least ⌈n/k⌉ items.
Example: In any group of 13 people, at least 2 share the same birth month (13 people, 12 months).

questions.question: How many 4-letter words can be formed from the letters of 'MATHEMATICS' with and without repetition?
keyPoints: MATHEMATICS has 11 letters with M(2), A(2), T(2), others once.
Without repetition: 11P4 = 11×10×9×8 = 7920 ways.
With repetition: 11×11×11×11 = 14641 ways (but must consider identical letters).
Actually: Using formula for permutations with repeated objects when all 4 used: Complex calculation needed.

questions.question: Find the number of ways to distribute 5 identical balls into 3 distinct boxes.
keyPoints: This is a 'stars and bars' problem.
Formula: (n+r-1)C(r-1) where n = balls, r = boxes
Verification: List combinations of 5 balls into 3 boxes

questions.question: In how many ways can 5 boys and 4 girls be seated in a row if (a) they can sit anywhere (b) no two girls sit together?
keyPoints: (a) Total 9 people = 9! = 362880 ways
(b) Arrange 5 boys first: 5! = 120 ways
4 gaps created: _ B _ B _ B _ B _
Choose 4 positions for girls: 4P4 or 4! = 24 ways
Total: 120 × 24 = 2880 ways

questions.question: Find the number of triangles that can be formed by joining the vertices of a regular polygon with n sides.
keyPoints: Total vertices = n
Choose any 3 vertices from n: nC3 triangles
But some selections form straight lines (degenerate triangles) for n ≥ 7
For convex polygon: All nC3 selections form valid triangles
Formula: nC3 = n(n-1)(n-2)/6

questions.question: Derive the formulas for nPr and nCr. Also prove that nPr = nCr × r!.
keyPoints: nPr = n!/(n-r)!: Choose r positions from n, arrange in order. First position: n choices, second: n-1, ..., rth: n-r+1. Product = n(n-1)...(n-r+1) = n!/(n-r)!
nCr = n!/(r!(n-r)!): First find permutations nPr, then divide by r! to remove order arrangements since combinations don't consider order.
Proof of nPr = nCr × r!: nCr × r! = [n!/(r!(n-r)!)] × r! = n!/(n-r)! = nPr. QED.

questions.question: Find the number of ways to arrange the letters of the word 'STATISTICS' and answer the following: (a) All letters used (b) Begins with S (c) All vowels together
keyPoints: STATISTICS has 10 letters: S(3), T(3), A(1), I(2), C(1)
(b) Fix S at first position: Remaining 9 letters: S(2), T(3), A(1), I(2), C(1) = 9!/(2!×3!×2!) = 362880/(2×6×2) = 15120

(c) Treat vowels together: Vowels = A,I,I (3 vowels with I repeated), fix as one block. Arrange: S,S,S,T,T,T,C + block = 7 items with S(3), T(3), C(1), block(1). Arrangements = 7!/(3!×3!) = 5040/36 = 140. Within block: 3!/2! = 3. Total = 140 × 3 = 420.

questions.question: (a) How many 5-digit numbers can be formed with digits 1,2,3,4,5,6 if digits may not be repeated and the number is divisible by 2? (b) If repetition is allowed, how many odd numbers less than 1000 can be formed using digits 1,2,3,4?
keyPoints: (a) For divisibility by 2, last digit must be even (2,4,6). Cases: Last=2: 5P4=120, Last=4: 120, Last=6: 120. Total = 360.
(b) 1-digit odd: 1,3 = 2. 2-digit odd: Units=1 or 3 (2 choices), Tens: 4 choices = 8. 3-digit odd: Units=2, Tens=4, Hundreds=4 = 16. Total = 2+8+16=26.

questions.question: Explain the 'Stars and Bars' method. In how many ways can 8 identical balls be distributed into (a) 3 distinct boxes (b) 3 identical boxes?
keyPoints: Stars and Bars: For distributing n identical items into r distinct boxes, use n+r-1 choose r-1 = (n+r-1)C(r-1).
(a) Distinct boxes: 8+3-1 choose 3-1 = 10C2 = 45 ways. Allow empty boxes.
